raw_response: {"rating": 3.5, "lean": "Right", "explanation": "This is a news report that consistently frames economic data through a pro-Trump, pro-administration lens, using celebratory framing and adopting administration talking points as the primary interpretive framework without counterbalancing perspectives. The accumulation of directional editorial choices warrants a Right classification. Detailed Analysis: (1) The headline uses 'Trump Boom' — an explicitly celebratory and politically branded phrase that is the author's own editorial choice, not a quotation, attributing economic success directly to the current president before any data is presented; (2) The phrase 'more than twice as many as expected' in the headline and lede is framed triumphantly rather than neutrally, with no acknowledgment of the wide forecast range until later in the piece; (3) The author independently characterizes the January numbers as 'extraordinary' in their own prose, an editorial judgment rather than a neutral description; (4) The author adopts the administration's 'reprivatize' framing without attribution or quotation marks, treating it as standard economic terminology rather than a political label; (5) The structural argument that lower job numbers now represent 'healthy—even robust—growth' is presented largely through unnamed economist consensus and administration-aligned sources (Navarro, Hassett), with no skeptical or opposing economic voices included; (6) The significant downward revisions revealing 800,000 fewer jobs than estimated — a substantial negative data point — are buried at the end of the article after extensive positive framing, minimizing their impact; (7) Source selection is entirely administration-friendly: Navarro on Fox Business and Hassett on CNBC are both White House advisers, and no independent or critical economic voices appear anywhere in the piece."}

parsed_output.explanation: This is a news report that consistently frames economic data through a pro-Trump, pro-administration lens, using celebratory framing and adopting administration talking points as the primary interpretive framework without counterbalancing perspectives. The accumulation of directional editorial choices warrants a Right classification. Detailed Analysis: (1) The headline uses 'Trump Boom' — an explicitly celebratory and politically branded phrase that is the author's own editorial choice, not a quotation, attributing economic success directly to the current president before any data is presented; (2) The phrase 'more than twice as many as expected' in the headline and lede is framed triumphantly rather than neutrally, with no acknowledgment of the wide forecast range until later in the piece; (3) The author independently characterizes the January numbers as 'extraordinary' in their own prose, an editorial judgment rather than a neutral description; (4) The author adopts the administration's 'reprivatize' framing without attribution or quotation marks, treating it as standard economic terminology rather than a political label; (5) The structural argument that lower job numbers now represent 'healthy—even robust—growth' is presented largely through unnamed economist consensus and administration-aligned sources (Navarro, Hassett), with no skeptical or opposing economic voices included; (6) The significant downward revisions revealing 800,000 fewer jobs than estimated — a substantial negative data point — are buried at the end of the article after extensive positive framing, minimizing their impact; (7) Source selection is entirely administration-friendly: Navarro on Fox Business and Hassett on CNBC are both White House advisers, and no independent or critical economic voices appear anywhere in the piece.

explanation: This is a news report that consistently frames economic data through a pro-Trump, pro-administration lens, using celebratory framing and adopting administration talking points as the primary interpretive framework without counterbalancing perspectives. The accumulation of directional editorial choices warrants a Right classification. Detailed Analysis: (1) The headline uses 'Trump Boom' — an explicitly celebratory and politically branded phrase that is the author's own editorial choice, not a quotation, attributing economic success directly to the current president before any data is presented; (2) The phrase 'more than twice as many as expected' in the headline and lede is framed triumphantly rather than neutrally, with no acknowledgment of the wide forecast range until later in the piece; (3) The author independently characterizes the January numbers as 'extraordinary' in their own prose, an editorial judgment rather than a neutral description; (4) The author adopts the administration's 'reprivatize' framing without attribution or quotation marks, treating it as standard economic terminology rather than a political label; (5) The structural argument that lower job numbers now represent 'healthy—even robust—growth' is presented largely through unnamed economist consensus and administration-aligned sources (Navarro, Hassett), with no skeptical or opposing economic voices included; (6) The significant downward revisions revealing 800,000 fewer jobs than estimated — a substantial negative data point — are buried at the end of the article after extensive positive framing, minimizing their impact; (7) Source selection is entirely administration-friendly: Navarro on Fox Business and Hassett on CNBC are both White House advisers, and no independent or critical economic voices appear anywhere in the piece.
